content.text: Atomic Agents is a lightweight Python framework built on top of

. Its central idea is that every agent interaction is a pure function from a typed input schema to a typed output schema — no hidden state, no magic, no string parsing. This makes agents easy to test, compose, and reason about.

Unlike heavier frameworks that build graph engines or orchestration runtimes on top of LLMs, Atomic Agents stays thin: it provides base classes for agents, tools, context, memory, and system prompts, then steps aside.

attrs.alt: Atomic Agents architecture: BaseAgent with typed Input/Output schemas, BaseContext, BaseSystemPrompt, BaseTool, and memory

content.text: Atomic Agents depends on Instructor for structured output extraction and Pydantic for schema definition. Install an LLM SDK alongside it — the examples below use the OpenAI SDK, but any client supported by Instructor works (Anthropic, Cohere, Mistral, etc.).
Every agent is parameterised with two Pydantic model classes that extend
. Instructor uses these schemas to force the LLM to produce valid, typed JSON rather than free text:

from atomic_agents import BaseAgent, BaseAgentConfig, BaseIOSchema
from instructor import from_openai
import openai

# Define typed input and output schemas
class QuestionInput(BaseIOSchema):
    """Input for the Q&A agent."""
    question: str

class AnswerOutput(BaseIOSchema):
    """Output from the Q&A agent."""
    answer: str
    confidence: float   # 0.0 – 1.0

# Create the client
client = from_openai(openai.OpenAI())

agent = BaseAgent(
    config=BaseAgentConfig(
        client=client,
        model="gpt-4o",
        input_schema=QuestionInput,
        output_schema=AnswerOutput,
    )
)

result = agent.run(QuestionInput(question="What is the speed of light?"))
print(result.answer, result.confidence)
annotations become part of the JSON schema that Instructor sends to the model. Well-named fields with clear descriptions significantly reduce parsing errors.

Instructor uses function-calling or structured-output mode depending on the provider. Pydantic validation runs on the parsed response; if it fails, Instructor automatically retries with the validation error as feedback.
method returns the system prompt string. Subclass it to inject runtime values — user preferences, language settings, domain expertise — without string interpolation scattered through your code:

from atomic_agents import BaseSystemPrompt
from pydantic import Field

class ResearcherPrompt(BaseSystemPrompt):
    """System prompt for a research assistant."""
    expertise: str = Field(default="general science")
    language: str = Field(default="English")

    def generate(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"You are an expert researcher specialising in {self.expertise}. "
            f"Always reply in {self.language}. "
            "Cite sources where possible and flag uncertain claims."
        )

agent = BaseAgent(
    config=BaseAgentConfig(
        client=client,
        model="gpt-4o",
        system_prompt=ResearcherPrompt(expertise="quantum physics"),
        input_schema=QuestionInput,
        output_schema=AnswerOutput,
    )
)
is a Pydantic model, you can validate, serialize, and version-control your system prompts the same way you do data models.

is a dataclass that holds everything an agent's tools and logic need from the surrounding application — database connections, feature flags, user identifiers. Pass it to the agent config and it is available to all registered tools at runtime:

from atomic_agents import BaseContext
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AppContext(BaseContext):
    db_url: str
    user_id: str
    max_results: int = 10

ctx = AppContext(db_url="postgresql://localhost/app", user_id="user-42")

agent = BaseAgent(
    config=BaseAgentConfig(
        client=client,
        model="gpt-4o",
        context=ctx,
        input_schema=QuestionInput,
        output_schema=AnswerOutput,
    )
)

The context is deliberately not passed through the input schema; keeping it separate makes the public API of the agent (what the caller supplies) distinct from its runtime environment (what the platform provides).
Tools in Atomic Agents follow the same schema-first pattern as agents. Define
, and register the tool with the context. Instructor ensures the model's tool-call arguments satisfy the input schema before

from atomic_agents import BaseTool, BaseToolInput, BaseToolOutput
from pydantic import Field

class WeatherInput(BaseToolInput):
    city: str = Field(description="The city to get weather for")

class WeatherOutput(BaseToolOutput):
    temperature: float
    condition: str

class WeatherTool(BaseTool):
    input_schema = WeatherInput
    output_schema = WeatherOutput
    name = "get_weather"
    description = "Get the current weather for a given city."

    def run(self, params: WeatherInput) -> WeatherOutput:
        # replace with a real API call
        return WeatherOutput(temperature=22.0, condition="Sunny")

# Tools are passed to the agent via context
ctx = AppContext(db_url="...", user_id="u1")
ctx.register_tool(WeatherTool())

agent = BaseAgent(
    config=BaseAgentConfig(
        client=client, model="gpt-4o", context=ctx,
        input_schema=QuestionInput, output_schema=AnswerOutput,
    )
)
Pydantic model validated from the LLM's tool-call arguments
Pydantic model returned by the tool and visible to the agent
Combines name, description, schemas, and the run() implementation
stores the conversation as a list of messages in-process. Set
to cap how many are retained, preventing unbounded context growth in long conversations:

from atomic_agents import BaseAgent, BaseAgentConfig, BaseIOSchema
from atomic_agents.memory import InMemoryMessageHistory

class ChatInput(BaseIOSchema):
    message: str

class ChatOutput(BaseIOSchema):
    reply: str

history = InMemoryMessageHistory(max_messages=20)

agent = BaseAgent(
    config=BaseAgentConfig(
        client=client,
        model="gpt-4o",
        memory=history,
        input_schema=ChatInput,
        output_schema=ChatOutput,
    )
)

agent.run(ChatInput(message="My name is Alice."))
agent.run(ChatInput(message="What is my name?"))  # recalls Alice
The history is passed to every
call automatically. For persistence across process restarts, replace
with a custom implementation that reads and writes to a database.
for incremental output. Each yielded value is a partially-constructed instance of the output schema, populated as the model generates tokens:

async def run_stream():
    async for partial in agent.stream(QuestionInput(question="Explain relativity.")):
        # partial is a partially-populated AnswerOutput instance
        print(partial.answer or "", end="", flush=True)

import asyncio
asyncio.run(run_stream())
Streaming requires an async context. The partial objects are not validated until the final
is complete, so treat them as read-only previews rather than guaranteed data.

Because agent outputs are Pydantic models, they can be used directly as inputs to other agents as long as the schemas match. This makes composition explicit and type-safe — the compiler catches schema mismatches before runtime:

This pattern scales to arbitrarily deep chains. Each agent has a single responsibility and a clear contract, which makes individual stages easy to swap (e.g. replace the researcher with a retrieval-based agent) without touching the writer.
The schema-first design makes unit testing straightforward. You can verify that outputs conform to the schema without a live LLM call, and mock the Instructor client to test agent logic in isolation:

from unittest.mock import patch

def test_agent_output():
    with patch.object(client, "chat") as mock_chat:
        mock_chat.completions.create.return_value = ...  # mock Instructor response
        result = agent.run(QuestionInput(question="Test?"))
    assert isinstance(result.answer, str)
    assert 0.0 <= result.confidence <= 1.0

# Because inputs and outputs are Pydantic models, you can construct and
# compare them directly without running an LLM:
expected = AnswerOutput(answer="Paris", confidence=0.99)
assert expected.answer == "Paris"
Testing the schema structure — field names, types, validators — independently of the LLM response quality catches a large class of bugs early, especially after refactoring.
Comparing Atomic Agents to Other Frameworks
fields without descriptions make it harder for the model to populate them correctly. Always use
Streaming yields partially validated objects. Accessing a field that has not yet been populated returns
rather than raising — add a null-check before using streamed partials.
lives in the agent's process memory. Scaling to multiple workers means each worker has its own isolated history. Implement a shared backend (Redis, Postgres) for multi-process deployments.
Agent output schemas must use JSON-serialisable types.
work; custom non-Pydantic classes do not — Instructor cannot force the model to produce a Python object directly.
Atomic Agents models every agent call as a pure function
with Pydantic schemas validated by Instructor.
subclasses define the typed contract of each agent; field descriptions guide the model.
encapsulates dynamic prompt logic in a version-controlled, testable Pydantic model.
injects database connections and runtime config without polluting the agent's public input schema.
Tools follow the same schema-first pattern:
provides sliding-window conversation history; replace it with a persistent backend for production.
Agents compose naturally: the output schema of one agent can serve as the input schema of the next when the types align.
The thin, explicit design makes unit testing straightforward without live LLM calls.
